title: [WIP] add image scan
body:
<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]".  That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.
-->
This is a draft implementation for issue #8305 (implement `podman scan` command).

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **NOT APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *wagoodman* To complete the [pull request process](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process), please assign **saschagrunert** after the PR has been reviewed. You can assign the PR to them by writing `/assign @saschagrunert` in a comment when ready. The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman).
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - **[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/OWNERS)** Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Continuing the conversation here:

> Things left todo:
> 
>     * make a PR in `github.com/containers/common` to expose a new configurable in `containers.conf`. I haven't identified where the change should be (maybe [here](https://github.com/containers/common/blob/master/pkg/config/config.go#L74)?) or what it may look like yet.

I think it should be part of the `[engine]` as it's rather something for Podman to interpret.  I think three knobs would make sense:
1. Image-scan image
2. Image-scan command
3. Image-scan mount point (i.e., where the image is being mounted)

> Questions:
> 
>     * are we OK with create/net commands being exposed on the scan command? I couldn't think of a reason not to include them.

I prefer to keep things as simple as possible.   Exposing all container run/create options can easily overwhelm users as it's not easy to find out which options relate to `image scan`.   I suggest to remove the run/create options and add them selectively if we need to in the future.

Thanks a lot for tackling this work, I really appreciate it!

It might be best to start with the man pages, to conclude the user experience.

$ podman image scan registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora

$ podman image scan --scanner quay.io/podman/scanner registry.fedoraproject.org/fedora

What does the user get as output?  Is there a scan report?   A json file?

We used to have the command `atomic scan`  can we steal something from there?

@vrothberg @rhatdan Thanks for the comments! I Found some time this afternoon to tackle some of this. I added a man page entry to show the basic behavior. I wanted to highlight a couple thoughts:

- regarding scanner input, the "standard" point for providing input would be for the tool image to respond to a `PODMAN_SCAN_MOUNT` environment variable. This doesn't seem ideal, but also seems better than assuming that the scanner takes positional arguments tailored to be a path to a directory. Any idea's on how to smooth this over a bit --keep it agnostic yet more flexibly configured?
- regarding the report output, given that the `podman image scan` command will be a generic spot to plugin various tooling then trying to normalize to a specific report format seems wrong. I feel that the tool should behave as freely as possible to express the results (even if say a TUI is brought up for the user to interact with). In this way if the tool is report-oriented then the user can run `podman image scan myimage:latest > report.txt` or similar to get the output. If the tool is acting as a quality gate, then `podman image scan myimage:latest` should exit with the same exit code as the scanner tool.
- regarding `atomic`, I'm not certain! I took a quick look and it wasn't apparent what could/should be pulled from that code base --let me know if you have some specific thoughts here.

Shout out if you had more thoughts / different ideas about the user experience!

Thanks, @wagoodman!  Really nice progress.

>     * regarding scanner input, the "standard" point for providing input would be for the tool image to respond to a `PODMAN_SCAN_MOUNT` environment variable. This doesn't seem ideal, but also seems better than assuming that the scanner takes positional arguments tailored to be a path to a directory. Any idea's on how to smooth this over a bit --keep it agnostic yet more flexibly configured?

I'd add another CLI flag (e.g., `--mount[-point]`).

>     * regarding the report output, given that the `podman image scan` command will be a generic spot to plugin various tooling then trying to normalize to a specific report format seems wrong. I feel that the tool should behave as freely as possible to express the results (even if say a TUI is brought up for the user to interact with). In this way if the tool is report-oriented then the user can run `podman image scan myimage:latest > report.txt` or similar to get the output. If the tool is acting as a quality gate, then `podman image scan myimage:latest` should exit with the same exit code as the scanner tool.

I agree.  Podman should just forward the output from the scanner.

>     * regarding `atomic`, I'm not certain! I took a quick look and it wasn't apparent what could/should be pulled from that code base --let me know if you have some specific thoughts here.

Can elaborate on "atomic"?  Not sure I can follow.  Maybe my "just returned from holidays" brain :)

@wagoodman, are you still working on it?

Please let us know in case you don't find time anymore. We can take the commit and kick it over the finish line.

@vrothberg I should be back on this next week, took a month off to welcome a new little one into the family 👶😊.

(Though, feel free to add any code to this PR!)

> @vrothberg I should be back on this next week, took a month off to welcome a new little one into the family babyblush.

Wonderful news, congratulations!

@vrothberg thanks! The little dude (Miles) is doing well and we're starting to get more rest with each passing day :)

I've also had some time to think about features of the `podman image scan` command a bit more:

1. It seems that since `podman image scan` is going to be agnostic to which tool is used, it could be expanded for the tool purpose as well. That is, the original idea was to mimic `docker scan` in terms of showing vulnerabilities, however, since the tooling doing the image scan is swappable it doesn't have to be constrained to a single use case. It would be way more useful if you could plug in different kinds of image scanners (e.g. vulnerabilities, secrets scanner, security linters).

2. The image mount approach works quite well, however, I think there is good reason to allow for additional image presentations to the scanner. 

   a. Since the image mount is the squashed representation of the image filesystem it doesn't show files that were removed in a previous layer but still exist in an inner-layer (say if built from a dockerfile) --this is important information for something like a secrets scanner, where the scanner needs to be able to see all files in all layers, even if they are not visible from filesystem of say a running container derived from the image in question.

   b. There are several scanners that already exist, and they take different formats as input: a plain filesystem as would be seen within a container instantiated from the image, a Docker tar archive from a `docker image save`, an OCI tar archive, an OCI directory layout. Since podman already has the ability to present the contents of an image in all of these formats it makes sense to allow for scanners in the `podman image scan` command to have access to the image in the format of choice.

3. The current prototype assumes that the scanner image needs to respond to the `PODMAN_SCAN_MOUNT ` environment variable --this really isn't ideal since the tool needs to change to be used as a `podman image scan` scanner. The problem is that there needs to be a way to feed configuration such as mountpoint and image format details into the scanner tool in a flexible fashion... I think I have a good way to express that, I'll update the manpage changes to reflect this, but the summary is to bring templating into arguments that are passed into the scanner.

   For instance, the `grype` tool is used as so when using a tar archive:
   ```
   docker image save -o image.tar myimage:latest

   grype docker-tar:./image.tar -o json
   ```

   It would be ideal to configure `podman image scan myimage:latest` to perform something along the lines of:
   ```
   podman image scan --mountpoint /tmp/image.tar --format docker-archive --scanner anchore/grype:latest myimage:latest -- 'docker-tar:{{ .Mountpoint }}' -o json
   ```

   Taking this as static configuration from disk would be most ideal, but allowing the options from the CLI should be first-class as well. 


Again, I'll update the branch as I prototype out these ideas. I'm interested in your feedback in the above points!

@vrothberg just pushed the man page updates + updated the code to reflect the changes. What's missing is how to provide all of these options via configuration (instead of needing to provide all of the options via command line every time). If we go this direction, would you know the best place for this configuration? (maybe somewhere in containers.conf?)

> @vrothberg thanks! The little dude (Miles) is doing well and we're starting to get more rest with each passing day :)

Wonderful!

> I've also had some time to think about features of the `podman image scan` command a bit more:
> 
>     1. It seems that since `podman image scan` is going to be agnostic to which tool is used, it could be expanded for the tool purpose as well. That is, the original idea was to mimic `docker scan` in terms of showing vulnerabilities, however, since the tooling doing the image scan is swappable it doesn't have to be constrained to a single use case. It would be way more useful if you could plug in different kinds of image scanners (e.g. vulnerabilities, secrets scanner, security linters).

Absolutely right!  I think that's worth mentioning in the docs that the "scanners" mustn't necessarily be limited to a security-only context.  Some scanners may want to list all installed .dep/rpm/python/js packages.

>     2. The image mount approach works quite well, however, I think there is good reason to allow for additional image presentations to the scanner.
>        a. Since the image mount is the squashed representation of the image filesystem it doesn't show files that were removed in a previous layer but still exist in an inner-layer (say if built from a dockerfile) --this is important information for something like a secrets scanner, where the scanner needs to be able to see all files in all layers, even if they are not visible from filesystem of say a running container derived from the image in question.

This seems like a quite advanced yet important use case.  I suggest starting with the simple use case of the fully expanded root FS first. We can extend it in the future.  It would require some plumbing in the containers/storage library.  But I really like the idea. @rhatdan PTAL

>        b. There are several scanners that already exist, and they take different formats as input: a plain filesystem as would be seen within a container instantiated from the image, a Docker tar archive from a `docker image save`, an OCI tar archive, an OCI directory layout. Since podman already has the ability to present the contents of an image in all of these formats it makes sense to allow for scanners in the `podman image scan` command to have access to the image in the format of choice.

Sounds good to me.  It should be easy to support as `podman run/create`, for instance, already support all these transports. So you can do `podman run oci:fedora ls`.

>     3. The current prototype assumes that the scanner image needs to respond to the `PODMAN_SCAN_MOUNT ` environment variable --this really isn't ideal since the tool needs to change to be used as a `podman image scan` scanner. The problem is that there needs to be a way to feed configuration such as mountpoint and image format details into the scanner tool in a flexible fashion... I think I have a good way to express that, I'll update the manpage changes to reflect this, but the summary is to bring templating into arguments that are passed into the scanner.
>        For instance, the `grype` tool is used as so when using a tar archive:
[...]
> Again, I'll update the branch as I prototype out these ideas. I'm interested in your feedback in the above points!

Feel free to update the PR and ping me.  I am interested in your ideas!
@wagoodman Are you still working on this?

@vrothberg 

> This seems like a quite advanced yet important use case. I suggest starting with the simple use case of the fully expanded root FS first.

re: supporting more than the root FS, the PR has this implemented already here https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/8706/files#diff-0d9d4382690094eec9c6d7dbe310c7a320686911170923af27d6d44a927603b0R82 (just by leveraging the ready-made `registry.ImageEngine().Save(...)`). Are we saying to take this out?

Also, regarding `#3` from https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/8706/files#issuecomment-776755461 , is the `containers.conf` the best place for this kind of this configuration? Or more generally, where should the configuration for this new "scan" options reside?

> @vrothberg
> 
> > This seems like a quite advanced yet important use case. I suggest starting with the simple use case of the fully expanded root FS first.
> 
> re: supporting more than the root FS, the PR has this implemented already here https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/8706/files#diff-0d9d4382690094eec9c6d7dbe310c7a320686911170923af27d6d44a927603b0R82 (just by leveraging the ready-made `registry.ImageEngine().Save(...)`). Are we saying to take this out?

I'd vote to take this out, yes.  We can add it in the future if there is a sufficiently big need for it.  In my experience, simplicity is the most important attribute in terms of usability and also maintainability.

> Also, regarding `#3` from https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/8706/files#issuecomment-776755461 , is the `containers.conf` the best place for this kind of this configuration? Or more generally, where should the configuration for this new "scan" options reside?

The link points to nirvana, so I miss some context.  `containers.conf` sounds like a good candidate in this context though.  But note that we can add support for containers.conf in a future PR.  That may help reduce the scope of the PR.

@vrothberg righto, I've removed the format option. Let me know if you think there is any missing functionality that we should be considering or other areas that need to be updated --this is probably ready to be taken out of draft and into final review.
A friendly reminder that this PR had no activity for 30 days.
Friendly ping.
@wagoodman, still working on this?

@vrothberg (and @cdoern ) friendly pong! I don't think I'll have cycles anytime in the near future to complete this unfortunately. Please feel free close this PR, continue this work without me, or open another one to take it's place 👍 .

> @vrothberg (and @cdoern ) friendly pong! I don't think I'll have cycles anytime in the near future to complete this unfortunately. Please feel free close this PR, continue this work without me, or open another one to take it's place +1 .

Thanks for your work on it!  I'll close the PR so others can finish.
